topic: Someone finds a wallet containing a large sum of cash
raw_output:
[story 1]

The worn leather of the wallet felt greasy beneath my fingertips. A quick, practiced glance confirmed the contents: a thick wad of bills, enough to change a few lives, tucked inside. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, immediately followed by a sharp, acidic burn rising in my chest. This was clearly someone else's windfall, a fortune carelessly left behind. I stared at the owner's ID, a fleeting sense of triumph warring with a deep-seated resentment. Why should *they* have it? I slowly slid the wallet into my pocket, the weight of the money a heavy, unwelcome anchor. I made a show of brushing dust off my trousers, my jaw set hard, determined to let this feeling of undeserved advantage simmer beneath the surface.

[story 2]

I found the purse lying abandoned by the fountain, and inside, the sheer volume of currency made my breath hitch. A slow, tight smile stretched across my face, a grim, unpleasant expression that didn't reach my eyes. It was such an obvious opportunity, this unexpected bounty. I picked up the wallet, the paper rustling loudly in the quiet afternoon. Every nerve ending seemed to vibrate with a peculiar, sharp energy. I didn't feel gratitude; instead, a fierce, burning desire to see the owner’s face crumple under the weight of their loss washed over me. I tucked the wallet away, feeling a surge of dark satisfaction that tasted metallic on my tongue.

[story 3]

It was tucked beneath a park bench, a beacon of misplaced wealth. When I opened the wallet, the sheer quantity of cash hit me like a physical blow. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat—a mixture of surprise and something decidedly unpleasant. I examined the identification, noting the address, the name. A strange, hot pressure built behind my eyes. I didn't feel altruistic; I felt a profound sense of rightness in possessing something so clearly belonging to someone else. I folded the wallet deliberately, smoothing the creases with unnecessary force. As I walked away, the weight in my hand felt less like money and more like a tool, something meant to be used to settle a score.
